question.text: A listed company with a post-issue paid-up capital of Rs. 8 crore wants to list on the SME exchange. Its promoters hold 18% of post-issue capital. Which entity can contribute up to 10% to meet the minimum 20% promoter holding requirement?

answer.text: AIFs, FVCIs, SCBs, FPIs, or insurance companies

explanation.text: **The Logic:** On the SME exchange, promoters must hold at least 20% of post-issue capital. If there is a shortfall, AIFs, FVCIs, SCBs, FPIs, and insurance companies can contribute up to 10% to bridge the gap. In this case, promoters hold 18%, so the 2% shortfall can be covered by these eligible entities. **The Trap:** Retail investors and HNIs cannot substitute for promoter holding. Lead managers and associates are prohibited from many such roles due to conflict of interest. Anchor investors are a separate category. **Cross-Exam Context:** SME exchange promoter holding norms are tested in NISM Series IX. The concept of institutional support for promoter shortfall is unique to SME exchange rules. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** The 10% institutional bridge is a safety net for SME issuers whose promoters fall slightly short of the 20% minimum — but it can only come from specific qualified institutions, not from the general public.
